title: Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method: World Champion Recipe ☕📈
description:
The 4:6 brewing method that made Tetsu Kasuya the 2016 World Brewers Cup champion. Split pours, taste tuning, and how 60% of the water determines strength.

Sources: Tetsu Kasuya YouTube, World Brewers Cup, James Hoffmann, Specialty Coffee Association brewing standards.

segments.voice: In 2016, a Japanese barista named Tetsu Kasuya won the World Brewers Cup with a pour-over method nobody had seen before. He called it the 4:6 method. The technique splits the brew into five separate pours with strategic intervals. The recipe transformed competitive brewing globally.

The 4:6 method gets its name from how it divides total brew water. The first forty percent of water shapes taste. The remaining sixty percent shapes strength. By manipulating each portion separately, brewers can tune the cup to specific flavor goals. The method is more flexible than continuous pour techniques.

Setting up the brew, Kasuya uses fifteen grams of medium-coarse ground coffee with two hundred fifty grams of water. The ratio of one to sixteen point seven sits between Hoffmann's one to sixteen and a lighter cup ratio. Water temperature ninety-three Celsius. V60 dripper with white paper filter.

The first forty percent of water, one hundred grams, splits into two pours. Pour fifty grams to bloom for the first forty-five seconds. The first pour wets the coffee evenly and releases CO2. Then pour another fifty grams between forty-five seconds and one minute thirty seconds.

Pouring less water in the first phase, like forty grams instead of fifty, produces a sweeter cup. Pouring more water in the first phase, like sixty grams, produces a more acidic cup. The split between the two pours within the first forty percent thus tunes the taste profile of the cup.

The remaining sixty percent of water, one hundred fifty grams, splits into three pours. Pour fifty grams at one minute thirty seconds. Pour fifty grams at two minutes fifteen seconds. Pour the final fifty grams at three minutes. The three pours control extraction strength.

Three pours produces a balanced cup. Two pours in the last sixty percent produces a stronger cup by extracting more in fewer rinses. Four or five pours in the last sixty percent produces a weaker cup. The number of pours determines the strength independently from the first phase taste tuning.

The total brew time should land around three minutes thirty seconds to three minutes forty-five seconds. If the brew drains faster than three minutes, the grind is too coarse. If the brew drains slower than four minutes, the grind is too fine. Adjust one notch at a time.

Kasuya recommends a medium-coarse grind, slightly coarser than Hoffmann's V60 setting. The coarser grind compensates for the longer total brew time produced by multiple pour intervals. The flow rate matters: water should drain between pours but not fully before the next pour begins.

The kettle pour technique matters as much as the timing. Kasuya pours in slow, steady spirals from center to edge and back, similar to Hoffmann. The pour rate should distribute water evenly across the coffee bed without disturbing the slurry too aggressively.

The 4:6 method works best with single-origin coffees rather than blends. The fine control over flavor allows brewers to highlight specific notes in delicate coffees. Ethiopian, Kenyan, and Colombian washed lots benefit most from the technique. Dark roast blends do not show the same dramatic improvement.

World Brewers Cup competition requires the contestant to design their own recipe. Kasuya developed the 4:6 method specifically for the 2016 competition. He used a washed Ethiopian coffee from Square Mile Coffee Roasters. His cup scored eighty-six on the judging scale, the highest of the competition.

After winning the championship, Kasuya opened his own brand and shop in Tokyo called Philocoffea. The brand exports drippers, kettles, and other brewing equipment globally. The Kasuya signature dripper has slightly modified geometry compared to standard V60, optimized for the 4:6 method.

Comparing 4:6 to Hoffmann V60, both produce excellent cups but with different characteristics. Hoffmann's two-pour method creates a slightly cleaner cup with consistent extraction. The 4:6 method allows more flavor tuning but requires more attention. Beginners often find 4:6 easier to adjust based on taste.

Tweaking the 4:6 method for taste: pour forty grams first then sixty grams in the first phase for a sweeter cup. Pour sixty grams first then forty grams for a brighter cup. The ratio between the two first-phase pours dramatically changes the cup character.

Tweaking the 4:6 method for strength: use two pours in the last sixty percent for stronger coffee. Use four pours for weaker coffee. Three pours produces a balanced extraction. The pours in the last phase do not need to be exactly equal in size, but they should remain similar.

Water quality matters as much for 4:6 as for any pour-over. Use water with one hundred fifty parts per million total dissolved solids, balanced calcium and magnesium. Chlorinated tap water destroys the delicate flavors that 4:6 is designed to reveal. Bottled water or filtered tap with adjustments works.

The 4:6 method scales for different brew sizes. For two cups, use thirty grams of coffee with five hundred grams of water, same ratios. For larger batches, the technique still works but requires longer total time and slightly coarser grind to handle the larger coffee bed.

Equipment for 4:6 brewing matches V60 setup. A V60 dripper, a paper filter, a server, a gooseneck kettle with variable temperature, a scale with timer, fresh coffee, and a burr grinder. The Kasuya signature dripper offers slight optimization but is not required.

The 4:6 method works on Chemex too, with adjustments. The thicker Chemex filter produces longer brew times. Reduce the number of pours in the last sixty percent to two for Chemex, totaling three pours including the first phase. The cup ends up cleaner due to filter thickness.

Different coffees benefit from different 4:6 tuning. Light roast florals like Ethiopian Yirgacheffe work best with sweet-tuning forty-grams-first first phase. Bright Kenyan AA tastes great with bright-tuning sixty-grams-first first phase. Match the tuning to the coffee character.

Brewing temperature affects 4:6 results. Kasuya recommends ninety-three Celsius. Slightly higher temperature, ninety-four to ninety-five Celsius, extracts more aggressively, useful for very light roasts. Slightly lower temperature, ninety-one to ninety-two, works better for darker roasts. Adjust within a small range.

The 4:6 method has limitations. The multi-pour structure takes longer than continuous pour methods. The brewer must stay attentive throughout the three to four minute brew. Distracted brewers will miss pour intervals and produce inconsistent results. Practice helps.

Tetsu Kasuya continues to develop new brewing techniques. His follow-up methods include the four-six immersion technique, the cold brew variations, and signature blends. His YouTube channel has over five hundred thousand subscribers globally. The 4:6 method remains his signature contribution.

But there is something pour-over enthusiasts rarely emphasize. The 4:6 method's main advantage over simpler methods is the ability to taste-tune. For someone who brews the same coffee every day, the simpler Hoffmann two-pour technique produces equally good results with less effort. The 4:6 shines when you brew different coffees and want to optimize each.

To master 4:6, start with the standard fifty-fifty first phase and three-pour second phase. Brew the same coffee for a week. Then experiment with sweet-tuning and strength-tuning variations. Tracking notes helps. The method rewards methodical practice. Next video, the AeroPress championship recipe that beat espresso. Subscribe.
